stem: A 62-year-old woman presents with a single episode of vaginal bleeding lasting 2 days, 9 years after her last menstrual period. She is not on hormone therapy. She has no abdominal pain, weight loss or vaginal discharge. She has a BMI of 33 kg/m2 and a history of type 2 diabetes on metformin. She is otherwise well and has had normal cervical screening for the last 10 years.

data_table.Examination: Speculum shows a healthy cervix with no visible lesion; bimanual examination reveals a non-tender, mobile, normal-sized uterus
data_table.Investigations: Pregnancy test not required; FBC normal; cervical screening up to date and HPV negative

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Repeat cervical screening test from the cervix and the vaginal vault now
options.rationale: Cervical screening is up to date and HPV negative; postmenopausal bleeding is investigated for endometrial pathology, not by repeating cervical screening. (Source: NCSP Australia 2023)

options.text: Outpatient hysteroscopy with Pipelle biopsy at the next available clinic
options.rationale: Hysteroscopy with biopsy is the second step after ultrasound shows endometrial thickness greater than 4 mm or persistent bleeding; performing it without prior ultrasound triage misallocates theatre time. (Source: RANZCOG Postmenopausal Bleeding)

options.text: Transvaginal ultrasound to measure the endometrial thickness now
options.rationale: Transvaginal ultrasound is the first-line investigation for postmenopausal bleeding; an endometrial thickness of 4 mm or less in a non-HRT user has a very high negative predictive value for endometrial cancer. (Source: RANZCOG Postmenopausal Bleeding)

options.text: Pelvic magnetic resonance imaging with intravenous contrast next week
options.rationale: Pelvic MRI is reserved for staging confirmed endometrial cancer or for problem-solving complex anatomy; it is not a first-line investigation for postmenopausal bleeding. (Source: RANZCOG Postmenopausal Bleeding)

options.text: Reassure that a single short bleed without other symptoms needs no work-up
options.rationale: Any postmenopausal bleeding warrants investigation because endometrial cancer accounts for around 10% of cases and is curable if detected early; reassurance without investigation is unsafe. (Source: RANZCOG Postmenopausal Bleeding)

explanation.summary: Postmenopausal bleeding is endometrial cancer until proven otherwise. The Australian first-line investigation is transvaginal ultrasound: endometrial thickness 4 mm or less in non-HRT users has a negative predictive value above 99%. Thicker endometrium or persistent bleeding triggers hysteroscopy and Pipelle biopsy. Risk factors include obesity, diabetes, tamoxifen, unopposed oestrogen, Lynch syndrome and nulliparity. Cervical screening is unrelated to this work-up.
key_points: Postmenopausal bleeding is endometrial cancer until proven otherwise
TVUS first; endometrial thickness 4 mm or less effectively rules out cancer in non-HRT users
Persistent bleeding or thickness above 4 mm goes to hysteroscopy + Pipelle biopsy
explanation.pearls: In Australian guidance the endometrial thickness threshold for proceeding to biopsy after postmenopausal bleeding is 4 mm in non-HRT users; this rises to around 5 mm in women on cyclical HRT.

stem: A 32-year-old G3P2 at 39+6 weeks is in established labour at 6 cm dilation. Oxytocin augmentation was commenced 90 minutes ago for slow progress. Over the last 20 minutes the CTG shows repetitive late decelerations to 90 beats per minute with reduced variability between contractions. Contractions are occurring every 90 seconds. The fetal heart rate baseline is 150. The midwife has called you and you are at the bedside.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 102/64 mmHg, HR 96, SpO2 99% in room air
data_table.Examination: Cervix 6 cm dilated, station -1, intact membranes; contractions every 90 seconds lasting 70 seconds
data_table.Investigations: CTG: repetitive late decelerations, reduced variability, baseline 150

lead_in: What is the single best initial intrauterine resuscitation step?

options.text: Increase the oxytocin infusion rate to strengthen contraction quality
options.rationale: Increasing oxytocin will worsen hyperstimulation and exacerbate late decelerations; the contraction interval of 90 seconds already exceeds tachysystole thresholds. (Source: RANZCOG IFS)

options.text: Proceed directly to category 1 caesarean section under general anaesthesia
options.rationale: Category 1 caesarean is reserved for fetal compromise that fails intrauterine resuscitation or for immediate threats such as cord prolapse, placental abruption or uterine rupture. (Source: RANZCOG IFS)

options.text: Administer subcutaneous terbutaline 250 mcg and continue oxytocin infusion
options.rationale: Terbutaline tocolysis is reserved for tachysystole that persists after stopping oxytocin, not as a first step while oxytocin still runs. (Source: RANZCOG IFS)

options.text: Stop the oxytocin infusion and reposition the woman to left lateral
options.rationale: Stopping uterine stimulation and left-lateral positioning relieve aortocaval compression and uterine hyperstimulation, the most common reversible causes of repetitive late decelerations during oxytocin augmentation. (Source: RANZCOG IFS)

options.text: Perform a fetal scalp lactate sample before any other intrauterine action
options.rationale: Fetal scalp sampling can complement decision-making but is not the first step; reversible causes such as hyperstimulation must be corrected first before invasive sampling. (Source: RANZCOG IFS)

explanation.summary: Intrauterine resuscitation addresses reversible causes of fetal compromise: aortocaval compression, hyperstimulation, hypotension and hypoxia. The standard sequence is stop oxytocin, reposition to left lateral, give intravenous fluid bolus, offer maternal oxygen if hypoxic, and review for cord prolapse or abruption. If hyperstimulation persists after oxytocin cessation, subcutaneous terbutaline 250 mcg is the next step. Failure to improve within 5 to 10 minutes triggers escalation to expedited delivery.
key_points: Always stop the oxytocin first when CTG worsens during augmentation
Persistent hyperstimulation after stopping oxytocin: terbutaline 250 mcg subcutaneously
explanation.pearls: In RANZCOG IFS terminology, tachysystole is more than 5 contractions in 10 minutes averaged over 30 minutes, and is the most common driver of repetitive late decelerations during oxytocin augmentation.

stem: A 30-year-old G1P0 attends for her dating ultrasound at 11+5 weeks. Two viable fetuses are seen with appropriate crown-rump lengths. You are reviewing the images with the sonographer. The placental territories are partly contiguous and a thin inter-twin membrane is visible.

data_table.Investigations: Two fetuses with CRL 48 and 50 mm, both with cardiac activity, inter-twin membrane visualised, placental masses contiguous

lead_in: Which single ultrasound finding most reliably establishes chorionicity at this gestation?

options.text: Two separate placental masses identified on a single ultrasound image
options.rationale: Apparently separate placentas may still be a single fused dichorionic placenta or even monochorionic with eccentric implantation, and so are not a reliable primary determinant of chorionicity. (Source: ISUOG Twin Practice Guideline)

options.text: Detection of two separate amniotic sacs around the twins on the scan
options.rationale: Two sacs differentiate diamniotic from monoamniotic but do not differentiate monochorionic from dichorionic; both dichorionic-diamniotic and monochorionic-diamniotic show two sacs. (Source: ISUOG Twin Practice Guideline)

options.text: Discordant fetal sex documented on transabdominal scanning at this gestation
options.rationale: Discordant sex confirms dichorionicity, but at 11 to 12 weeks fetal sex is not reliably visible on transabdominal scanning; it cannot be the primary determinant at this gestation. (Source: ISUOG Twin Practice Guideline)

options.text: Inter-twin membrane thickness measured greater than 2 mm at the midpoint
options.rationale: Membrane thickness becomes less reliable as gestation advances and is a poor stand-alone discriminator at 12 weeks compared with the lambda or T sign. (Source: ISUOG Twin Practice Guideline)

options.text: Lambda twin-peak sign at the inter-twin membrane placental insertion now
options.rationale: At 10 to 14 weeks the lambda (twin peak) sign at the placental insertion of the inter-twin membrane reliably indicates a dichorionic pregnancy with a positive predictive value above 95%; the T sign indicates monochorionic. (Source: ISUOG Twin Practice Guideline)

explanation.summary: Chorionicity is the single most important determinant of twin pregnancy management because monochorionic pregnancies carry markedly higher rates of twin-twin transfusion, selective growth restriction and perinatal loss. The most reliable first-trimester sign is the lambda (twin peak) sign at the placental insertion of the inter-twin membrane, present in dichorionic pregnancies; the T sign indicates monochorionic. After 14 weeks these signs become less reliable, so chorionicity should be documented and labelled on every dating scan in twin pregnancy.
key_points: Lambda sign at placental insertion of membrane = dichorionic
T sign at placental insertion = monochorionic
Document chorionicity at the dating scan; signs become unreliable after 14 weeks
explanation.pearls: The lambda sign loses sensitivity beyond 14 weeks because chorionic regression renders the placental wedge thin; this is why every Australian twin pregnancy must have chorionicity recorded before 14 weeks.

stem: A 34-year-old woman returns to her general practitioner for her cervical screening result. She is well, sexually active and uses a copper IUD for contraception. The cervical screening test reports oncogenic HPV detected, type 16, with reflex liquid-based cytology showing possible high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion. She has had previous normal results.

data_table.Investigations: HPV: oncogenic type 16 detected; LBC: possible HSIL; previous CSTs normal 2.5 years ago

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Repeat the cervical screening test in 12 months from today's date
options.rationale: Repeat HPV testing at 12 months is the surveillance pathway for non-16/18 oncogenic HPV with normal or low-grade cytology, not for HPV16 with high-grade cytology. (Source: NCSP Australia 2023)

options.text: Refer to colposcopy for assessment and directed biopsy without delay
options.rationale: Oncogenic HPV16 with possible HSIL on reflex cytology is a direct referral to colposcopy under the NCSP 2023 pathway, regardless of previous result history. (Source: NCSP Australia 2023)

options.text: Arrange a large loop excision of the transformation zone (LLETZ) procedure
options.rationale: LLETZ is performed only after histological confirmation by colposcopy-directed biopsy or when see-and-treat is appropriate at colposcopy with senior judgement, not as a first community step. (Source: NCSP Australia 2023)

options.text: Order pelvic magnetic resonance imaging for cervical staging this week
options.rationale: Pelvic MRI is reserved for staging biopsy-confirmed invasive cervical cancer; it is not an initial screening abnormality work-up step. (Source: NCSP Australia 2023)

options.text: Remove the copper intrauterine device prior to any further evaluation
options.rationale: The copper IUD does not need to be removed before colposcopy and removing it adds nothing to evaluation of HPV-related cytological abnormality. (Source: NCSP Australia 2023)

explanation.summary: The 2023 National Cervical Screening Program in Australia stratifies management by HPV genotype and reflex cytology. Oncogenic HPV16 or 18 detection at any cytology grade triggers direct colposcopy referral because of higher cervical cancer risk per infection. Other oncogenic HPV with negative or pLSIL cytology enters 12-month surveillance; with HSIL or higher it also goes to colposcopy. Definitive treatment (LLETZ, cone biopsy, hysterectomy) follows histological confirmation. The copper IUD does not affect the screening pathway.
key_points: HPV16/18 at any cytology grade -> colposcopy referral
Other oncogenic HPV + pLSIL/negative cytology -> 12-month repeat HPV
Other oncogenic HPV + HSIL or higher -> colposcopy referral
explanation.pearls: HPV16 alone carries an absolute 10-year cumulative cervical cancer risk above 10% if untreated, which is why the 2023 NCSP pathway sends HPV16-positive women to colposcopy regardless of cytology grade.

stem: A 33-year-old G3P2 at 32+0 weeks presents with sudden painless bright red vaginal bleeding that filled half a pad in 20 minutes and has now slowed. She has had two previous caesarean sections. The fetal movement chart shows normal movement. She has no contractions and no abdominal pain. She is haemodynamically stable on arrival but anxious.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 112/68 mmHg, HR 96, SpO2 99% in room air, Temp 36.6 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, fundal height appropriate, fetal heart rate 145 with normal variability, no contractions, NO digital vaginal examination performed
data_table.Investigations: Blood group A Rh-positive, Hb 116 g/L, platelets 245, group and hold sent; routine 20-week ultrasound noted placenta covering the internal cervical os

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Sterile speculum examination after transabdominal ultrasound now
options.rationale: In suspected placenta praevia, digital vaginal examination is contraindicated; sterile speculum is permitted after ultrasound confirmation of placental location to assess for local causes. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia)

options.text: Digital vaginal examination to assess cervical dilation today
options.rationale: Digital vaginal examination is contraindicated in suspected placenta praevia because it can precipitate catastrophic haemorrhage. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia)

options.text: Intramuscular ergometrine 250 micrograms to control the bleeding
options.rationale: Uterotonics are not used in antepartum haemorrhage from placenta praevia; they have no role until after delivery and can precipitate maternal hypertension. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia)

options.text: Immediate elective caesarean section under spinal anaesthesia today
options.rationale: At 32 weeks, with bleeding now slowing and a stable mother and fetus, conservative inpatient management with corticosteroids is preferred to immediate delivery, which would commit a preterm infant to NICU. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia)

options.text: Discharge home with general practitioner review in 48 hours plus pad chart
options.rationale: Active or recent antepartum haemorrhage at 32 weeks with previous caesareans and praevia requires inpatient observation, intravenous access, group and hold, and consideration of corticosteroids for fetal lung maturation. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia)

explanation.summary: Antepartum haemorrhage with suspected or known placenta praevia requires admission, IV access, group and hold (consider cross-match if heavy), continuous CTG, no digital vaginal examination, and transabdominal ultrasound to confirm placental location. Sterile speculum is permitted after imaging to assess for local causes. Corticosteroids are given at 24+0 to 34+6 weeks for fetal lung maturation. Two prior caesareans plus praevia raises the risk of placenta accreta spectrum and warrants tertiary review. Bleeding that persists, escalates or compromises mother or fetus mandates expedited delivery by caesarean.
key_points: No digital vaginal examination in suspected praevia
Speculum after ultrasound confirms placental location
Antenatal corticosteroids at 24+0 to 34+6 weeks when delivery is possible within 7 days
explanation.pearls: Placenta praevia plus two or more previous caesareans confers an accreta spectrum risk of around 40%; these women need tertiary multidisciplinary planning, including consideration of caesarean hysterectomy and interventional radiology standby.
